title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
Lecture 18 — Final Lecture
India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
Bringing It All Together






Eighteen weeks ago, we asked:

Why do countries trade? And what does that mean for the 600 million Indians who depend on agriculture for their livelihoods?

Today, we can answer that question fully — with theory, data, institutions, and policy.
We have traced the journey from Ricardo’s simple insight about comparative advantage, through the architecture of WTO rules, the mechanics of import tariffs, the politics of MSP and food security, the diplomacy of phytosanitary protocols, and the paperwork of a Shipping Bill on ICEGATE.
This is what ECO 2203 has been about.


What we built, week by week:

Weeks 1–2: Why trade? Gains and features
Weeks 3–6: Theory — Ricardo to Heckscher-Ohlin to New Trade Theory
Weeks 7–8: Protectionism — tariffs, quotas, subsidies
Weeks 9–10: Balance of Payments and India’s external sector
Weeks 11–12: Foreign exchange markets
Weeks 13–14: WTO, AoA, and India’s negotiating positions
Week 15: SPS barriers — India’s compliance challenge
Weeks 16–17: Export procedures and organizations
Week 18: Today — synthesis, FTP, and vision 2030









Course Synthesis: International Trade Logic Chain

Comparative Advantage → countries gain from specialisation
Gains from Trade → mediated by terms of trade
Trade Policy → tariffs, quotas, subsidies shape distribution
BOP & Exchange Rates → macro context for trade flows
International Institutions → WTO, AoA, SPS discipline policy
India’s Strategy → Export promotion + selective protection



Every lecture has been one step in this chain.
The central tension of the entire course:
Free trade maximises efficiency — comparative advantage theory, consumer gains, global welfare.
But agriculture is not like other sectors — food is existential, farming communities are politically powerful, and market failures (information asymmetry, price volatility, public goods in R&D) are pervasive.
The discipline of agricultural trade economics is, fundamentally, the study of how societies navigate this tension.






library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

df_tl <- data.frame(
  Year  = c(1991, 1995, 2000, 2004, 2005, 2015, 2020, 2023),
  Label = c("LPG\nReforms","WTO\nMembership","EXIM Bank\nExpansion",
            "FTP 2004-09","SEZ Act","FTP 2015-20","Atmanirbhar\nBharat","FTP 2023-28"),
  Type  = c("Structural","Multilateral","Finance","Policy","Investment","Policy","Policy","Policy"),
  y_seg = c(0.60, -0.62, 0.45, -0.50, 0.72, -0.65, 0.52, -0.72),
  x_lab = c(1991, 1995, 2000, 2001.5, 2007, 2015, 2020, 2023)
)
type_cols <- c(Structural="#012169",Multilateral="#C84B31",Finance="#B9975B",Policy="#4a7fd4",Investment="#2a8a5c")
ggplot(df_tl) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey50", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_segment(aes(x=Year,xend=Year,y=0,yend=y_seg*0.72,colour=Type),linewidth=1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(x=Year,y=0,colour=Type),size=4.5) +
  geom_label(aes(x=x_lab,y=y_seg,label=Label,fill=Type),colour="white",size=2.7,fontface="bold",
             label.padding=unit(0.22,"lines"),label.r=unit(0.12,"lines")) +
  geom_text(aes(x=Year,y=ifelse(y_seg>0,-0.14,0.14),label=Year),size=2.7,colour="grey35") +
  scale_colour_manual(values=type_cols) + scale_fill_manual(values=type_cols) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(1986,2028),ylim=c(-1.0,1.0)) +
  labs(title="India's Foreign Trade Policy: Key Milestones (1991–2023)",
       subtitle="Coloured by event type  |  Alternating above/below timeline",
       x=NULL,y=NULL,colour="Type",fill="Type") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(axis.text=element_blank(),axis.ticks=element_blank(),panel.grid=element_blank(),
        plot.title=element_text(face="bold"),plot.subtitle=element_text(colour="grey40"),legend.position="bottom")







Figure 1: India’s trade policy evolved from the 1991 LPG reforms through WTO accession and successive FTPs, culminating in the landmark FTP 2023-28 targeting USD 2 trillion by 2030. Source: DGFT, Ministry of Commerce and Industry, GoI.











India’s agricultural trade balance (FY2024):




FY2024
FY2022 (Record)




Agri Exports
$43.7 billion
$53.1 billion


Agri Imports
$28.2 billion
$31.0 billion


Trade Surplus
$15.5 billion
$22.1 billion



India’s global rank:

9th largest agricultural exporter globally (WTO, 2024)
Agriculture = 11% of India’s total merchandise exports
India accounts for ~2.5% of global agricultural trade

Ten-year trend (FY2015–FY2024):
FY2015: $33B → FY2019: $38B → FY2022: $53B (record) → FY2023: $53B → FY2024: $43.7B
Decline post-FY2022: rice export ban (Aug 2023) removed ~$6B of rice export revenue; global commodity price correction.


What explains the FY2022 record?

Post-pandemic food commodity price spike: global wheat, rice, edible oil prices hit multi-decade highs
Russia-Ukraine war: major exporters disrupted; India stepped in for wheat (March–May 2022)
Basmati boom: Gulf demand surge
Shrimp expansion: Andhra aquaculture at peak output

What explains the FY2024 decline?

Non-basmati rice export ban (August 2023) — removed 4M MT from pipeline
Global commodity price correction — palm oil, wheat down 30–40% from peaks
Cotton price decline — Bt cotton bollworm resistance raised costs

Despite the decline, $43.7B remains the 3rd highest ever. The long-run trajectory is strongly upward.









India’s agri export basket (FY2024, ~$43.7B):

Total rice = $12.2B = 28% of agricultural exports.
India accounts for 40%+ of global rice trade. This is market power — but also concentration risk.


The rice concentration problem:
When India restricts rice exports (as in 2023), global prices spike — India is price-setter, not price-taker for rice. This creates:

Geopolitical responsibility: Bangladesh, Senegal, Indonesia depend on Indian rice for food security
Policy credibility risk: export bans damage India’s reputation as reliable supplier
Revenue volatility: One policy decision swings India’s agri export earnings by $3–4B


Lesson for FTP: India needs to diversify its export basket — reduce rice’s 28% share to below 20% by growing processed food, spices, marine, and organic exports. Over-dependence on any single commodity creates fragility in both domestic policy and international trade relationships.










India’s agri export destinations (FY2024):



Destination
Share

10%
Rice, meat, fruits, spices

Growing markets:

Japan and South Korea: Organic, premium spices, basmati — high unit value
East Africa: Rice and pulses — growing demand as urbanisation accelerates
Gulf (Oman, Qatar, Kuwait): Processed food and dairy demand rising



The India-UAE CEPA advantage (May 2022):
India’s first significant FTA with agricultural chapters — a model for future agreements.
Agricultural gains for India under CEPA:

Basmati rice: zero duty (was 5%)
Seafood: zero duty on 60+ species (was 5–7%)
Mango, banana, dates: duty-free
Processed food: phased tariff reduction to zero

FY2023 result: India-UAE agri trade up 28% in first year of CEPA.
Future FTAs being negotiated:

India-UK FTA: Could unlock premium seafood, spice, organic, and basmati access to post-Brexit UK market
India-EU FTA: Most complex but highest value — EU is world’s largest agri importer; GI recognition critical


FTAs with agricultural chapters are the next frontier for Indian agri exports. The gains from CEPA show what is possible.










India’s agri imports (FY2024, ~$28.2B):



Product
Value
Risk




Palm oil (Indonesia/Malaysia)
$9.5B
Critical


Soybean oil (Argentina/Brazil)
$2.5B
High


Sunflower oil (Ukraine/Russia)
$2.2B
High


Pulses (Canada, Myanmar, Australia)
$2.8B
Moderate


Fresh fruits (USA, South Africa)
$1.8B
Low


Cashew (West Africa)
$1.4B
Low


Spices (Vietnam, Indonesia)
$0.8B
Low



The edible oil problem:
India imports ~14 million MT of edible oil — 65% of consumption. Total oil import bill: ~$14.2 billion. Three countries (Indonesia, Malaysia, Argentina) supply 90% of this.
India’s agricultural trade surplus would be $29.7 billion without the oil import drag.


Why edible oil dependence is a structural vulnerability:

Food security risk: Indonesia’s palm oil export ban (April–May 2022) caused immediate price spike in Indian households; sunflower oil crisis (Russia-Ukraine war, 2022)
Forex drain: $14.2B per year leaves India’s current account
Geopolitical leverage: Malaysia used palm oil access as bargaining chip after India’s Kashmir statement criticism (2019)

NMEO-OP (National Mission on Edible Oils — Oil Palm, 2021):

₹11,040 crore over 5 years
Target: expand oil palm to 10 lakh ha (Northeast + Andaman)
Current: ~3.5 lakh ha
Progress: slow — land availability constraints, 3–4 year gestation, farmer preference for annual crops


Edible oil import dependence is India’s single largest agri trade vulnerability. Domestic oilseed yield improvement is slower than demand growth — the structural dependency may persist for 10–15 years despite policy intervention.










India overwhelmingly exports raw or semi-processed agricultural commodities. Three case studies in value-addition loss:
1. Cashew: India is the world’s 2nd largest raw cashew producer. But raw cashew nuts are exported to Vietnam and Brazil for processing. Vietnam processes, packages as W320/W180 grade, sells globally at 2.5–3× the raw price. India loses processing jobs, value-added revenue, and brand ownership.
2. Turmeric: India exports dried turmeric (bulk commodity): $200M per year. Curcumin extract (the active compound) sells for $30–80 per kg vs $2/kg for raw turmeric. Japanese and German companies extract, encapsulate, and sell health supplements globally at 5–10× the raw material value. India captures only the raw material margin.
3. Shrimp: India exports mostly raw frozen shrimp. Breaded, marinated, stuffed, ready-to-cook formats command a 35–50% price premium over commodity-grade frozen shrimp. MPEDA’s target: double value-added seafood from 18% to 35% of export mix by 2030.


The value-chain imperative:
Moving up the value chain delivers:

Higher export revenue per tonne
More skilled processing and packaging jobs
Brand equity: Indian turmeric supplements, Indian ready-to-eat meals, Indian flavoured cashews
Reduced vulnerability to raw commodity price cycles

FTP 2023–28 response:

RTE (Ready-to-Eat) meal exports as priority sector
Functional foods and nutraceuticals: turmeric capsules, ashwagandha extracts
PM FME scheme: formalise 2 lakh micro food enterprises → FSSAI compliant → export-ready
ODOP + GI: premium branding of regional specialty products


India’s processed food export target: $10B by 2027 (from $4.8B in FY2024). The MOFPI’s Mega Food Parks — 24 operational as of FY2024 — are India’s vehicle for industrial-scale food processing.










India’s FTP 2023–28 was released on March 31, 2023 by the Ministry of Commerce.
Vision: India’s total merchandise + services exports to reach $2 trillion by 2030 (from ~$775B in FY2023).
The four strategic themes:

Incentive to Remission: Move from export subsidies (WTO-incompatible) to remission of embedded taxes — RoDTEP as core instrument
Collaboration: EPCs, industry associations, Export Houses, start-up exporters
Ease of Doing Business: DARPAN platform (real-time incentive tracking), paperless documentation
Emerging Areas: Millets, organic, e-commerce exports, services integration


FTP 2023–28 is India’s first policy explicitly targeting e-commerce exports — a revolutionary departure from goods-only thinking. Target: $200B in e-commerce merchandise exports by 2030.



Agricultural provisions in FTP 2023–28:

Districts as Export Hubs (DEH): 100+ agri-focused districts; District Export Promotion Committees; product-specific action plans
One District One Product (ODOP): Brand and promote one signature product per district nationally and internationally
International Year of Millets alignment: Position India as global millet hub
GI Promotion: Increased support for registrations and international brand protection
RoDTEP rates maintained: 0.5–4.3% for agri products
TMA extended: Transport and Marketing Assistance — freight subsidy on perishables and processed food
APEDA mandate expanded: Covers millet-based products, organic, and functional foods

Amnesty scheme: 45,000 exporters with unresolved EPCG/Advance Authorisation obligations given one-time settlement — clearing the compliance backlog that had frozen export incentives for thousands of SME exporters.








library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

df_ftp <- data.frame(
  Sector=c("Merchandise","Services","Agri & Allied","Textiles","Pharma","Engineering","Electronics","Chemicals"),
  Target_2030_B =c(1000,1000,100,100,130,350,300,300),
  Current_FY24_B=c(437,341,43.7,35,28,109,23,28)
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(Sector=factor(Sector,levels=Sector[order(Current_FY24_B)])) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols=c(Current_FY24_B,Target_2030_B),names_to="Type",values_to="Value_B") |>
  dplyr::mutate(Type=ifelse(Type=="Current_FY24_B","FY2024 Actual","FTP 2030 Target"))
ggplot(df_ftp,aes(x=Value_B,y=Sector,fill=Type)) +
  geom_col(position=position_dodge(width=0.72),width=0.64) +
  annotate("text",x=700,y=8.4,label="Total target: USD 2 trillion by 2030",
           colour="#C84B31",size=3.1,fontface="bold",hjust=0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("FY2024 Actual"="#012169","FTP 2030 Target"="#B9975B")) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks=c(10,30,100,300,1000),labels=function(x) paste0("$",x,"B")) +
  labs(title="India FTP 2023-28: Sector Export Targets vs FY2024 Actuals (USD Billion)",
       subtitle="Log scale  |  Gold = FTP 2030 target; Blue = FY2024 actual",
       x="Export Value (USD Billion, log scale)",y=NULL,fill=NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(legend.position="top",plot.title=element_text(face="bold"),plot.subtitle=element_text(colour="grey40"))







Figure 2: India’s FTP 2023-28 targets USD 2 trillion in total exports by 2030. Agri and Allied targets USD 100B, more than doubling the FY2024 actual of USD 43.7B. Log scale used for wide value range. Source: DGFT, Foreign Trade Policy 2023-28.










library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

scheme_lv <- c("MEIS (2015-20)","RoDTEP (2021-current)")
fc <- c("MEIS (2015-20)"="#C84B31","RoDTEP (2021-current)"="#012169")
df_prod <- data.frame(Scheme=scheme_lv,Value=c(8000,10500))
df_out  <- data.frame(Scheme=scheme_lv,Value=c(45000,17000))
df_exp  <- data.frame(Scheme=scheme_lv,Value=c(60,85))
p1 <- ggplot(df_prod,aes(x=Scheme,y=Value,fill=Scheme)) +
  geom_col(width=0.52,show.legend=FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label=formatC(Value,format="d",big.mark=",")),vjust=-0.5,size=3.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=fc) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,13500),labels=function(x) formatC(x,format="d",big.mark=",")) +
  labs(title="Products Covered",x=NULL,y="No. of Products") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10)+theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",hjust=0.5))
p2 <- ggplot(df_out,aes(x=Scheme,y=Value,fill=Scheme)) +
  geom_col(width=0.52,show.legend=FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label=paste0("\u20b9",formatC(Value,format="d",big.mark=",")," Cr")),vjust=-0.5,size=3.1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=fc) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,56000),labels=function(x) paste0("\u20b9",formatC(x,format="d",big.mark=","))) +
  labs(title="\u20b9 Outlay (Crore)",x=NULL,y="\u20b9 Crore") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10)+theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",hjust=0.5))
p3 <- ggplot(df_exp,aes(x=Scheme,y=Value,fill=Scheme)) +
  geom_col(width=0.52,show.legend=FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label=paste0(Value,"K")),vjust=-0.5,size=3.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=fc) + scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,105)) +
  labs(title="Exporters Benefited",x=NULL,y="Thousands") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10)+theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",hjust=0.5))
(p1|p2|p3)+plot_annotation(
  title="MEIS vs RoDTEP: India's Export Incentive Schemes Compared",
  subtitle="Note: RoDTEP is WTO-compliant (tax refund)  |  MEIS was challenged at WTO (DS541)",
  theme=theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",size=12),plot.subtitle=element_text(colour="grey40",size=9)))







Figure 3: RoDTEP covers more products and exporters than MEIS but at lower outlay, reflecting its design as a WTO-compliant tax remission rather than a direct subsidy (MEIS was challenged at WTO as DS541). Source: DGFT; WTO DS541 dispute record.











The Shri Anna (millet) revolution:
India proposed and the UN General Assembly declared 2023 as the International Year of Millets — a diplomatic success for Indian agriculture that positioned millets as a global priority crop.
India’s millet portfolio:



Millet
Hindi Name
Key States




Pearl millet
Bajra
Rajasthan, Gujarat, Haryana


Sorghum
Jowar
Maharashtra, Karnataka


Finger millet
Ragi
Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh


Foxtail millet
Kangni
Telangana, Andhra Pradesh


Kodo millet
Kodo
Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh



Why millets, why now:

Nutritional superfood trend: High protein, high fibre, gluten-free — resonates with urban health consumers in Europe, USA, Gulf
Climate resilience: 30–50% less water than rice and wheat; suits arid and semi-arid lands
India’s absolute advantage: Largest producer globally; deep indigenous variety diversity



APEDA’s Millet Export Program:

Target: $100M exports by FY2025 (from ~$64M in FY2023)
72 countries introduced to Indian millets at IYM2023 events
Special pavilions at Gulfood Dubai, SIAL Paris, Annapoorna showcasing millet products: cookies, pasta, snack bars, flour blends, ready-to-eat porridge

Commercial success stories:

Tata Sampann millet range: Available on Amazon USA and UK
24 Mantra Organic: Organic millet exports to EU and USA, $5M+
Grains of India: DTC millet brand targeting Indian diaspora in UK and USA

PM’s global diplomacy:
PM Modi personally served millet-based dishes to G20 leaders at the 2023 New Delhi Summit — country branding for Indian millets at the highest level.

Global health food market size for millets: $11 billion by 2028 growing at 4.5% CAGR. India has first-mover advantage — but must invest in processing, packaging, and certification to convert agricultural abundance into export earnings.










India’s GI landscape (2024):

Total GIs registered: 400+
Agricultural GIs: 230+ (largest category)
40 new GIs registered in FY2024

Marquee agricultural GIs:



Product
State
Price Premium




Darjeeling Tea
West Bengal
40–60%


Basmati Rice
Punjab/UP belt
35–50%


Alphonso Mango
Maharashtra (Ratnagiri)
50–80%


Malabar Black Pepper
Kerala
25–35%


Alleppey Green Cardamom
Kerala
30–40%


Guntur Sannam Chilli
Andhra Pradesh
20–30%


Coorg Arabica Coffee
Karnataka
40–60%


Kolhapuri Chilli
Maharashtra/Karnataka
25–35%



A GI is, economically, a geographic monopoly on a name. Producers in the designated region capture the premium; imitators cannot legally use the name in protected jurisdictions.


The Basmati GI battle:
India’s application to protect “Basmati” as a GI at EU has been contentious:

India’s claim: Only rice from designated districts of Punjab, Haryana, UP, Uttarakhand, J&K qualifies
Pakistan’s objection: Pakistani basmati (Super Kernel, Kainat varieties) has equal claim; both countries should be co-holders
Stakes: If EU grants India-only GI, Pakistan loses ~$1B annual EU basmati market access
Decision pending 2024–25

Why GIs matter for development:
Darjeeling tea’s GI protection means the 87 estates and their workers capture the “Darjeeling” brand premium — which reaches £20–40 per 100g in UK specialty retail — directly benefiting the 500,000+ workers in those gardens.

GI policy is distribution policy — it determines who captures the value premium from cultural and geographic heritage.










1. SPS compliance — the entry ticket to premium markets:

EU rejections of Indian spices (EtO contamination, 2023): Everest and MDH spice brands recalled in Singapore, Hong Kong, USA; reputational damage to all Indian spice exporters
Aflatoxin in groundnuts, ochratoxin in coffee: recurring EU border rejections
FDA import alerts on Indian shrimp for antibiotic residues

Required investment: Farmer-level GAP training, village-level residue testing, cold chain to reduce mycotoxin development.
2. Value addition gap:
India’s agri export unit values are consistently below global averages because we export raw and semi-processed commodities. Until the processing sector scales, India will underperform its potential.
3. Export restriction credibility:
Rice bans (2023), wheat ban (2022), sugar quotas (2022), onion bans (2023) — India has a pattern of imposing sudden export restrictions to manage domestic inflation. Each restriction: - Damages India’s image as a reliable supplier - Disrupts long-term trade relationships - Benefits domestic consumers short-term but hurts farmers

4. Edible oil import dependence:
$14.2B annual import drag; NMEO-OP progress slow; structural vulnerability to supply disruptions and price spikes.
5. Cold chain and logistics:
35M MT cold storage vs 90M MT requirement; 25–40% post-harvest losses; logistics cost 14% of GDP vs 8% in advanced economies.
6. Market concentration:
Rice at 28% of agri exports; USA + UAE + Bangladesh = 32% of destinations. Shocks in any one product or market reverberate through the whole system.

The meta-challenge:
India’s domestic agricultural policy is designed primarily for food security and farmer welfare — these are legitimate and important goals. But they frequently conflict with the objectives of export competitiveness and trade reliability. Until India develops better institutional mechanisms to reconcile these tensions, export performance will remain hostage to domestic policy volatility.









Despite the challenges, India faces an extraordinary window of opportunity:
1. Rising global food demand:
World population reaches 10 billion by 2050; global food demand projected to rise 50% from 2012 levels. India’s agricultural land, labour, and knowledge base positions it as a key supplier.
2. Indian diaspora — the ready market:
$4B+ in annual food imports by Indian diaspora communities globally (USA, UK, Canada, Australia, Gulf). Indian diaspora is the most rapidly growing food import segment in UK supermarkets (Tesco, Waitrose Indian ranges up 35% in 5 years). This is a market that wants Indian products and pays premiums.
3. Organic and clean-label premium markets:
Global organic food market: $220B and growing 10% annually. India has 4.5 million organic farmers — the world’s largest base — but captures only 0.5% of global organic market. The upside potential is immense.
4. Millets and superfoods:
First-mover advantage in a fast-growing $11B global segment. India can own the “Shri Anna” narrative globally.


5. FTA market access (India-UAE CEPA live; India-UK FTA pending):
Each FTA opens a new premium market without tariff headwinds. India-UAE CEPA showed 28% trade growth in year one.
6. ASEAN’s growing middle class:
Southeast Asia’s middle class (Indonesia, Vietnam, Philippines, Thailand) is 400M+ and growing. Rising income = rising demand for diverse, higher-quality foods including Indian spices, rice varieties, processed snacks.
7. Africa’s rice gap:
Sub-Saharan Africa imports $7B in rice annually. India supplies 40%. With logistics investment (AIC — Africa-India Connectivity), India can deepen its dominant position in Africa’s food import market.

The opportunity is real. But converting opportunity into sustained export growth requires policy stability, infrastructure investment, quality compliance, and institutional capacity. All of which we have studied in this course.










This dilemma has surfaced in almost every lecture:
The MSP–export nexus:
MSP raises domestic procurement prices → good for farmers → but may price Indian exports above global levels, reducing competitiveness.
India’s wheat MSP in 2023–24: ₹2,275/quintal (~$274/MT). International wheat price (Chicago): ~$220/MT. Result: Indian wheat exports not competitive without subsidies.
The food security–export reliability tension:
When domestic food prices rise, India bans or restricts exports to protect consumers. But:

Farmer incomes fall (they lose export premium)
India’s trade partners face sudden supply shock
Long-term trade relationships damaged
India’s reputation as reliable supplier eroded

The fiscal cost of support:
India’s food subsidy bill: ₹2.05 lakh crore (FY2024). FCI procurement, PDS distribution, buffer stocks → massive public expenditure. This is AMS under WTO’s Amber Box — India is approaching WTO limits.


There are no easy answers — only trade-offs:








Policy Goal
Instrument
Trade-off




Farmer income
High MSP
Fiscal cost; export uncompetitiveness


Consumer welfare
Export ban
Farmer income loss; trade reputation


Export growth
Remove export tax
May raise domestic food prices


WTO compliance
Reduce AMS
Farmer lobby resistance


Food security
Import restriction
Higher domestic prices; consumer loss




The key insight from ECO 2203:
Trade policy in agriculture is inherently about distributing gains and losses among farmers, consumers, traders, and government. There is no free lunch. Understanding these trade-offs — not choosing one side blindly — is what makes an economist valuable to society.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
Eighteen weeks ago, we asked:

Why do countries trade? And what does that mean for the 600 million Indians who depend on agriculture for their livelihoods?

Today, we can answer that question fully — with theory, data, institutions, and policy.
We have traced the journey from Ricardo’s simple insight about comparative advantage, through the architecture of WTO rules, the mechanics of import tariffs, the politics of MSP and food security, the diplomacy of phytosanitary protocols, and the paperwork of a Shipping Bill on ICEGATE.
This is what ECO 2203 has been about.


What we built, week by week:

Weeks 1–2: Why trade? Gains and features
Weeks 3–6: Theory — Ricardo to Heckscher-Ohlin to New Trade Theory
Weeks 7–8: Protectionism — tariffs, quotas, subsidies
Weeks 9–10: Balance of Payments and India’s external sector
Weeks 11–12: Foreign exchange markets
Weeks 13–14: WTO, AoA, and India’s negotiating positions
Week 15: SPS barriers — India’s compliance challenge
Weeks 16–17: Export procedures and organizations
Week 18: Today — synthesis, FTP, and vision 2030

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
Course Synthesis: International Trade Logic Chain

Comparative Advantage → countries gain from specialisation
Gains from Trade → mediated by terms of trade
Trade Policy → tariffs, quotas, subsidies shape distribution
BOP & Exchange Rates → macro context for trade flows
International Institutions → WTO, AoA, SPS discipline policy
India’s Strategy → Export promotion + selective protection



Every lecture has been one step in this chain.
The central tension of the entire course:
Free trade maximises efficiency — comparative advantage theory, consumer gains, global welfare.
But agriculture is not like other sectors — food is existential, farming communities are politically powerful, and market failures (information asymmetry, price volatility, public goods in R&D) are pervasive.
The discipline of agricultural trade economics is, fundamentally, the study of how societies navigate this tension.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

df_tl <- data.frame(
  Year  = c(1991, 1995, 2000, 2004, 2005, 2015, 2020, 2023),
  Label = c("LPG\nReforms","WTO\nMembership","EXIM Bank\nExpansion",
            "FTP 2004-09","SEZ Act","FTP 2015-20","Atmanirbhar\nBharat","FTP 2023-28"),
  Type  = c("Structural","Multilateral","Finance","Policy","Investment","Policy","Policy","Policy"),
  y_seg = c(0.60, -0.62, 0.45, -0.50, 0.72, -0.65, 0.52, -0.72),
  x_lab = c(1991, 1995, 2000, 2001.5, 2007, 2015, 2020, 2023)
)
type_cols <- c(Structural="#012169",Multilateral="#C84B31",Finance="#B9975B",Policy="#4a7fd4",Investment="#2a8a5c")
ggplot(df_tl) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey50", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_segment(aes(x=Year,xend=Year,y=0,yend=y_seg*0.72,colour=Type),linewidth=1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(x=Year,y=0,colour=Type),size=4.5) +
  geom_label(aes(x=x_lab,y=y_seg,label=Label,fill=Type),colour="white",size=2.7,fontface="bold",
             label.padding=unit(0.22,"lines"),label.r=unit(0.12,"lines")) +
  geom_text(aes(x=Year,y=ifelse(y_seg>0,-0.14,0.14),label=Year),size=2.7,colour="grey35") +
  scale_colour_manual(values=type_cols) + scale_fill_manual(values=type_cols) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(1986,2028),ylim=c(-1.0,1.0)) +
  labs(title="India's Foreign Trade Policy: Key Milestones (1991–2023)",
       subtitle="Coloured by event type  |  Alternating above/below timeline",
       x=NULL,y=NULL,colour="Type",fill="Type") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(axis.text=element_blank(),axis.ticks=element_blank(),panel.grid=element_blank(),
        plot.title=element_text(face="bold"),plot.subtitle=element_text(colour="grey40"),legend.position="bottom")







Figure 1: India’s trade policy evolved from the 1991 LPG reforms through WTO accession and successive FTPs, culminating in the landmark FTP 2023-28 targeting USD 2 trillion by 2030. Source: DGFT, Ministry of Commerce and Industry, GoI.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
India’s agricultural trade balance (FY2024):




FY2024
FY2022 (Record)




Agri Exports
$43.7 billion
$53.1 billion


Agri Imports
$28.2 billion
$31.0 billion


Trade Surplus
$15.5 billion
$22.1 billion



India’s global rank:

9th largest agricultural exporter globally (WTO, 2024)
Agriculture = 11% of India’s total merchandise exports
India accounts for ~2.5% of global agricultural trade

Ten-year trend (FY2015–FY2024):
FY2015: $33B → FY2019: $38B → FY2022: $53B (record) → FY2023: $53B → FY2024: $43.7B
Decline post-FY2022: rice export ban (Aug 2023) removed ~$6B of rice export revenue; global commodity price correction.


What explains the FY2022 record?

Post-pandemic food commodity price spike: global wheat, rice, edible oil prices hit multi-decade highs
Russia-Ukraine war: major exporters disrupted; India stepped in for wheat (March–May 2022)
Basmati boom: Gulf demand surge
Shrimp expansion: Andhra aquaculture at peak output

What explains the FY2024 decline?

Non-basmati rice export ban (August 2023) — removed 4M MT from pipeline
Global commodity price correction — palm oil, wheat down 30–40% from peaks
Cotton price decline — Bt cotton bollworm resistance raised costs

Despite the decline, $43.7B remains the 3rd highest ever. The long-run trajectory is strongly upward.

Total rice = $12.2B = 28% of agricultural exports.
India accounts for 40%+ of global rice trade. This is market power — but also concentration risk.


The rice concentration problem:
When India restricts rice exports (as in 2023), global prices spike — India is price-setter, not price-taker for rice. This creates:

Geopolitical responsibility: Bangladesh, Senegal, Indonesia depend on Indian rice for food security
Policy credibility risk: export bans damage India’s reputation as reliable supplier
Revenue volatility: One policy decision swings India’s agri export earnings by $3–4B


Lesson for FTP: India needs to diversify its export basket — reduce rice’s 28% share to below 20% by growing processed food, spices, marine, and organic exports. Over-dependence on any single commodity creates fragility in both domestic policy and international trade relationships.

Growing markets:

Japan and South Korea: Organic, premium spices, basmati — high unit value
East Africa: Rice and pulses — growing demand as urbanisation accelerates
Gulf (Oman, Qatar, Kuwait): Processed food and dairy demand rising



The India-UAE CEPA advantage (May 2022):
India’s first significant FTA with agricultural chapters — a model for future agreements.
Agricultural gains for India under CEPA:

Basmati rice: zero duty (was 5%)
Seafood: zero duty on 60+ species (was 5–7%)
Mango, banana, dates: duty-free
Processed food: phased tariff reduction to zero

FY2023 result: India-UAE agri trade up 28% in first year of CEPA.
Future FTAs being negotiated:

India-UK FTA: Could unlock premium seafood, spice, organic, and basmati access to post-Brexit UK market
India-EU FTA: Most complex but highest value — EU is world’s largest agri importer; GI recognition critical


FTAs with agricultural chapters are the next frontier for Indian agri exports. The gains from CEPA show what is possible.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
India’s agri imports (FY2024, ~$28.2B):



Product
Value
Risk




Palm oil (Indonesia/Malaysia)
$9.5B
Critical


Soybean oil (Argentina/Brazil)
$2.5B
High


Sunflower oil (Ukraine/Russia)
$2.2B
High


Pulses (Canada, Myanmar, Australia)
$2.8B
Moderate


Fresh fruits (USA, South Africa)
$1.8B
Low


Cashew (West Africa)
$1.4B
Low


Spices (Vietnam, Indonesia)
$0.8B
Low



The edible oil problem:
India imports ~14 million MT of edible oil — 65% of consumption. Total oil import bill: ~$14.2 billion. Three countries (Indonesia, Malaysia, Argentina) supply 90% of this.
India’s agricultural trade surplus would be $29.7 billion without the oil import drag.


Why edible oil dependence is a structural vulnerability:

Food security risk: Indonesia’s palm oil export ban (April–May 2022) caused immediate price spike in Indian households; sunflower oil crisis (Russia-Ukraine war, 2022)
Forex drain: $14.2B per year leaves India’s current account
Geopolitical leverage: Malaysia used palm oil access as bargaining chip after India’s Kashmir statement criticism (2019)

NMEO-OP (National Mission on Edible Oils — Oil Palm, 2021):

₹11,040 crore over 5 years
Target: expand oil palm to 10 lakh ha (Northeast + Andaman)
Current: ~3.5 lakh ha
Progress: slow — land availability constraints, 3–4 year gestation, farmer preference for annual crops


Edible oil import dependence is India’s single largest agri trade vulnerability. Domestic oilseed yield improvement is slower than demand growth — the structural dependency may persist for 10–15 years despite policy intervention.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
India overwhelmingly exports raw or semi-processed agricultural commodities. Three case studies in value-addition loss:
1. Cashew: India is the world’s 2nd largest raw cashew producer. But raw cashew nuts are exported to Vietnam and Brazil for processing. Vietnam processes, packages as W320/W180 grade, sells globally at 2.5–3× the raw price. India loses processing jobs, value-added revenue, and brand ownership.
2. Turmeric: India exports dried turmeric (bulk commodity): $200M per year. Curcumin extract (the active compound) sells for $30–80 per kg vs $2/kg for raw turmeric. Japanese and German companies extract, encapsulate, and sell health supplements globally at 5–10× the raw material value. India captures only the raw material margin.
3. Shrimp: India exports mostly raw frozen shrimp. Breaded, marinated, stuffed, ready-to-cook formats command a 35–50% price premium over commodity-grade frozen shrimp. MPEDA’s target: double value-added seafood from 18% to 35% of export mix by 2030.


The value-chain imperative:
Moving up the value chain delivers:

Higher export revenue per tonne
More skilled processing and packaging jobs
Brand equity: Indian turmeric supplements, Indian ready-to-eat meals, Indian flavoured cashews
Reduced vulnerability to raw commodity price cycles

FTP 2023–28 response:

RTE (Ready-to-Eat) meal exports as priority sector
Functional foods and nutraceuticals: turmeric capsules, ashwagandha extracts
PM FME scheme: formalise 2 lakh micro food enterprises → FSSAI compliant → export-ready
ODOP + GI: premium branding of regional specialty products


India’s processed food export target: $10B by 2027 (from $4.8B in FY2024). The MOFPI’s Mega Food Parks — 24 operational as of FY2024 — are India’s vehicle for industrial-scale food processing.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
India’s FTP 2023–28 was released on March 31, 2023 by the Ministry of Commerce.
Vision: India’s total merchandise + services exports to reach $2 trillion by 2030 (from ~$775B in FY2023).
The four strategic themes:

Incentive to Remission: Move from export subsidies (WTO-incompatible) to remission of embedded taxes — RoDTEP as core instrument
Collaboration: EPCs, industry associations, Export Houses, start-up exporters
Ease of Doing Business: DARPAN platform (real-time incentive tracking), paperless documentation
Emerging Areas: Millets, organic, e-commerce exports, services integration


FTP 2023–28 is India’s first policy explicitly targeting e-commerce exports — a revolutionary departure from goods-only thinking. Target: $200B in e-commerce merchandise exports by 2030.



Agricultural provisions in FTP 2023–28:

Districts as Export Hubs (DEH): 100+ agri-focused districts; District Export Promotion Committees; product-specific action plans
One District One Product (ODOP): Brand and promote one signature product per district nationally and internationally
International Year of Millets alignment: Position India as global millet hub
GI Promotion: Increased support for registrations and international brand protection
RoDTEP rates maintained: 0.5–4.3% for agri products
TMA extended: Transport and Marketing Assistance — freight subsidy on perishables and processed food
APEDA mandate expanded: Covers millet-based products, organic, and functional foods

Amnesty scheme: 45,000 exporters with unresolved EPCG/Advance Authorisation obligations given one-time settlement — clearing the compliance backlog that had frozen export incentives for thousands of SME exporters.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

df_ftp <- data.frame(
  Sector=c("Merchandise","Services","Agri & Allied","Textiles","Pharma","Engineering","Electronics","Chemicals"),
  Target_2030_B =c(1000,1000,100,100,130,350,300,300),
  Current_FY24_B=c(437,341,43.7,35,28,109,23,28)
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(Sector=factor(Sector,levels=Sector[order(Current_FY24_B)])) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols=c(Current_FY24_B,Target_2030_B),names_to="Type",values_to="Value_B") |>
  dplyr::mutate(Type=ifelse(Type=="Current_FY24_B","FY2024 Actual","FTP 2030 Target"))
ggplot(df_ftp,aes(x=Value_B,y=Sector,fill=Type)) +
  geom_col(position=position_dodge(width=0.72),width=0.64) +
  annotate("text",x=700,y=8.4,label="Total target: USD 2 trillion by 2030",
           colour="#C84B31",size=3.1,fontface="bold",hjust=0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("FY2024 Actual"="#012169","FTP 2030 Target"="#B9975B")) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks=c(10,30,100,300,1000),labels=function(x) paste0("$",x,"B")) +
  labs(title="India FTP 2023-28: Sector Export Targets vs FY2024 Actuals (USD Billion)",
       subtitle="Log scale  |  Gold = FTP 2030 target; Blue = FY2024 actual",
       x="Export Value (USD Billion, log scale)",y=NULL,fill=NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(legend.position="top",plot.title=element_text(face="bold"),plot.subtitle=element_text(colour="grey40"))







Figure 2: India’s FTP 2023-28 targets USD 2 trillion in total exports by 2030. Agri and Allied targets USD 100B, more than doubling the FY2024 actual of USD 43.7B. Log scale used for wide value range. Source: DGFT, Foreign Trade Policy 2023-28.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

scheme_lv <- c("MEIS (2015-20)","RoDTEP (2021-current)")
fc <- c("MEIS (2015-20)"="#C84B31","RoDTEP (2021-current)"="#012169")
df_prod <- data.frame(Scheme=scheme_lv,Value=c(8000,10500))
df_out  <- data.frame(Scheme=scheme_lv,Value=c(45000,17000))
df_exp  <- data.frame(Scheme=scheme_lv,Value=c(60,85))
p1 <- ggplot(df_prod,aes(x=Scheme,y=Value,fill=Scheme)) +
  geom_col(width=0.52,show.legend=FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label=formatC(Value,format="d",big.mark=",")),vjust=-0.5,size=3.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=fc) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,13500),labels=function(x) formatC(x,format="d",big.mark=",")) +
  labs(title="Products Covered",x=NULL,y="No. of Products") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10)+theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",hjust=0.5))
p2 <- ggplot(df_out,aes(x=Scheme,y=Value,fill=Scheme)) +
  geom_col(width=0.52,show.legend=FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label=paste0("\u20b9",formatC(Value,format="d",big.mark=",")," Cr")),vjust=-0.5,size=3.1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=fc) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,56000),labels=function(x) paste0("\u20b9",formatC(x,format="d",big.mark=","))) +
  labs(title="\u20b9 Outlay (Crore)",x=NULL,y="\u20b9 Crore") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10)+theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",hjust=0.5))
p3 <- ggplot(df_exp,aes(x=Scheme,y=Value,fill=Scheme)) +
  geom_col(width=0.52,show.legend=FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label=paste0(Value,"K")),vjust=-0.5,size=3.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=fc) + scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,105)) +
  labs(title="Exporters Benefited",x=NULL,y="Thousands") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=10)+theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",hjust=0.5))
(p1|p2|p3)+plot_annotation(
  title="MEIS vs RoDTEP: India's Export Incentive Schemes Compared",
  subtitle="Note: RoDTEP is WTO-compliant (tax refund)  |  MEIS was challenged at WTO (DS541)",
  theme=theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",size=12),plot.subtitle=element_text(colour="grey40",size=9)))







Figure 3: RoDTEP covers more products and exporters than MEIS but at lower outlay, reflecting its design as a WTO-compliant tax remission rather than a direct subsidy (MEIS was challenged at WTO as DS541). Source: DGFT; WTO DS541 dispute record.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
The Shri Anna (millet) revolution:
India proposed and the UN General Assembly declared 2023 as the International Year of Millets — a diplomatic success for Indian agriculture that positioned millets as a global priority crop.
India’s millet portfolio:



Millet
Hindi Name
Key States




Pearl millet
Bajra
Rajasthan, Gujarat, Haryana


Sorghum
Jowar
Maharashtra, Karnataka


Finger millet
Ragi
Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh


Foxtail millet
Kangni
Telangana, Andhra Pradesh


Kodo millet
Kodo
Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh



Why millets, why now:

Nutritional superfood trend: High protein, high fibre, gluten-free — resonates with urban health consumers in Europe, USA, Gulf
Climate resilience: 30–50% less water than rice and wheat; suits arid and semi-arid lands
India’s absolute advantage: Largest producer globally; deep indigenous variety diversity



APEDA’s Millet Export Program:

Target: $100M exports by FY2025 (from ~$64M in FY2023)
72 countries introduced to Indian millets at IYM2023 events
Special pavilions at Gulfood Dubai, SIAL Paris, Annapoorna showcasing millet products: cookies, pasta, snack bars, flour blends, ready-to-eat porridge

Commercial success stories:

Tata Sampann millet range: Available on Amazon USA and UK
24 Mantra Organic: Organic millet exports to EU and USA, $5M+
Grains of India: DTC millet brand targeting Indian diaspora in UK and USA

PM’s global diplomacy:
PM Modi personally served millet-based dishes to G20 leaders at the 2023 New Delhi Summit — country branding for Indian millets at the highest level.

Global health food market size for millets: $11 billion by 2028 growing at 4.5% CAGR. India has first-mover advantage — but must invest in processing, packaging, and certification to convert agricultural abundance into export earnings.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
India’s GI landscape (2024):

Total GIs registered: 400+
Agricultural GIs: 230+ (largest category)
40 new GIs registered in FY2024

Marquee agricultural GIs:



Product
State
Price Premium




Darjeeling Tea
West Bengal
40–60%


Basmati Rice
Punjab/UP belt
35–50%


Alphonso Mango
Maharashtra (Ratnagiri)
50–80%


Malabar Black Pepper
Kerala
25–35%


Alleppey Green Cardamom
Kerala
30–40%


Guntur Sannam Chilli
Andhra Pradesh
20–30%


Coorg Arabica Coffee
Karnataka
40–60%


Kolhapuri Chilli
Maharashtra/Karnataka
25–35%



A GI is, economically, a geographic monopoly on a name. Producers in the designated region capture the premium; imitators cannot legally use the name in protected jurisdictions.


The Basmati GI battle:
India’s application to protect “Basmati” as a GI at EU has been contentious:

India’s claim: Only rice from designated districts of Punjab, Haryana, UP, Uttarakhand, J&K qualifies
Pakistan’s objection: Pakistani basmati (Super Kernel, Kainat varieties) has equal claim; both countries should be co-holders
Stakes: If EU grants India-only GI, Pakistan loses ~$1B annual EU basmati market access
Decision pending 2024–25

Why GIs matter for development:
Darjeeling tea’s GI protection means the 87 estates and their workers capture the “Darjeeling” brand premium — which reaches £20–40 per 100g in UK specialty retail — directly benefiting the 500,000+ workers in those gardens.

GI policy is distribution policy — it determines who captures the value premium from cultural and geographic heritage.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
1. SPS compliance — the entry ticket to premium markets:

EU rejections of Indian spices (EtO contamination, 2023): Everest and MDH spice brands recalled in Singapore, Hong Kong, USA; reputational damage to all Indian spice exporters
Aflatoxin in groundnuts, ochratoxin in coffee: recurring EU border rejections
FDA import alerts on Indian shrimp for antibiotic residues

Required investment: Farmer-level GAP training, village-level residue testing, cold chain to reduce mycotoxin development.
2. Value addition gap:
India’s agri export unit values are consistently below global averages because we export raw and semi-processed commodities. Until the processing sector scales, India will underperform its potential.
3. Export restriction credibility:
Rice bans (2023), wheat ban (2022), sugar quotas (2022), onion bans (2023) — India has a pattern of imposing sudden export restrictions to manage domestic inflation. Each restriction: - Damages India’s image as a reliable supplier - Disrupts long-term trade relationships - Benefits domestic consumers short-term but hurts farmers

4. Edible oil import dependence:
$14.2B annual import drag; NMEO-OP progress slow; structural vulnerability to supply disruptions and price spikes.
5. Cold chain and logistics:
35M MT cold storage vs 90M MT requirement; 25–40% post-harvest losses; logistics cost 14% of GDP vs 8% in advanced economies.
6. Market concentration:
Rice at 28% of agri exports; USA + UAE + Bangladesh = 32% of destinations. Shocks in any one product or market reverberate through the whole system.

The meta-challenge:
India’s domestic agricultural policy is designed primarily for food security and farmer welfare — these are legitimate and important goals. But they frequently conflict with the objectives of export competitiveness and trade reliability. Until India develops better institutional mechanisms to reconcile these tensions, export performance will remain hostage to domestic policy volatility.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
Despite the challenges, India faces an extraordinary window of opportunity:
1. Rising global food demand:
World population reaches 10 billion by 2050; global food demand projected to rise 50% from 2012 levels. India’s agricultural land, labour, and knowledge base positions it as a key supplier.
2. Indian diaspora — the ready market:
$4B+ in annual food imports by Indian diaspora communities globally (USA, UK, Canada, Australia, Gulf). Indian diaspora is the most rapidly growing food import segment in UK supermarkets (Tesco, Waitrose Indian ranges up 35% in 5 years). This is a market that wants Indian products and pays premiums.
3. Organic and clean-label premium markets:
Global organic food market: $220B and growing 10% annually. India has 4.5 million organic farmers — the world’s largest base — but captures only 0.5% of global organic market. The upside potential is immense.
4. Millets and superfoods:
First-mover advantage in a fast-growing $11B global segment. India can own the “Shri Anna” narrative globally.


5. FTA market access (India-UAE CEPA live; India-UK FTA pending):
Each FTA opens a new premium market without tariff headwinds. India-UAE CEPA showed 28% trade growth in year one.
6. ASEAN’s growing middle class:
Southeast Asia’s middle class (Indonesia, Vietnam, Philippines, Thailand) is 400M+ and growing. Rising income = rising demand for diverse, higher-quality foods including Indian spices, rice varieties, processed snacks.
7. Africa’s rice gap:
Sub-Saharan Africa imports $7B in rice annually. India supplies 40%. With logistics investment (AIC — Africa-India Connectivity), India can deepen its dominant position in Africa’s food import market.

The opportunity is real. But converting opportunity into sustained export growth requires policy stability, infrastructure investment, quality compliance, and institutional capacity. All of which we have studied in this course.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
This dilemma has surfaced in almost every lecture:
The MSP–export nexus:
MSP raises domestic procurement prices → good for farmers → but may price Indian exports above global levels, reducing competitiveness.
India’s wheat MSP in 2023–24: ₹2,275/quintal (~$274/MT). International wheat price (Chicago): ~$220/MT. Result: Indian wheat exports not competitive without subsidies.
The food security–export reliability tension:
When domestic food prices rise, India bans or restricts exports to protect consumers. But:

Farmer incomes fall (they lose export premium)
India’s trade partners face sudden supply shock
Long-term trade relationships damaged
India’s reputation as reliable supplier eroded

The fiscal cost of support:
India’s food subsidy bill: ₹2.05 lakh crore (FY2024). FCI procurement, PDS distribution, buffer stocks → massive public expenditure. This is AMS under WTO’s Amber Box — India is approaching WTO limits.


There are no easy answers — only trade-offs:








Policy Goal
Instrument
Trade-off




Farmer income
High MSP
Fiscal cost; export uncompetitiveness


Consumer welfare
Export ban
Farmer income loss; trade reputation


Export growth
Remove export tax
May raise domestic food prices


WTO compliance
Reduce AMS
Farmer lobby resistance


Food security
Import restriction
Higher domestic prices; consumer loss




The key insight from ECO 2203:
Trade policy in agriculture is inherently about distributing gains and losses among farmers, consumers, traders, and government. There is no free lunch. Understanding these trade-offs — not choosing one side blindly — is what makes an economist valuable to society.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
Course Synthesis: Module by Module


Module 1 — Trade Theory (Lectures 1–6):
Comparative advantage explains why India exports basmati, spices, seafood, and cotton — all labour-intensive, land-intensive, or knowledge-intensive goods where India’s resource endowment gives a production cost advantage. The Heckscher-Ohlin theorem predicts India exports labour-intensive goods; the New Trade Theory (Krugman) explains why India also imports and exports processed food simultaneously.
Module 2 — Trade Policy (Lectures 7–8):
India’s tariff structure (avg. agri applied: 36.4%, bound: 113.5%) creates massive “water” for policy flexibility. Export subsidies under WTO scrutiny led India to shift from MEIS to RoDTEP. MSP creates domestic support that exceeds WTO Amber Box limits — India’s most sensitive ongoing WTO compliance challenge.
Module 3 — BoP and External Sector (Lectures 9–10):
Agriculture contributes a net current account surplus of $15.5B — providing vital forex earnings. The BoP constraint (Lectures 9–10) shows how India’s agricultural export earnings help finance industrial imports.

Module 4 — Foreign Exchange (Lectures 11–12):
The rupee’s depreciation trend (₹65/$1 in 2015 → ₹83/$1 in 2024) has boosted rupee realisation for Indian agri exporters — a natural export incentive. But it also raises the rupee cost of edible oil imports. Forex management affects every agri exporter and importer.
Module 5 — WTO and AoA (Lectures 13–14):
WTO rules constrain AND protect. The AoA’s AMS limits constrain India’s food subsidy policies. But the Special and Differential Treatment (S&DT) provisions, Peace Clause, and Developing Country flexibilities give India significant policy space. India’s G-33 and G-20 coalitions at WTO are crucial for protecting this space.
Module 6 — SPS and Quality (Lecture 15):
SPS compliance is the entry ticket to premium markets. EU, USA, Japan — the highest-value markets — have stringent residue and hygiene standards. India’s FSSAI, EIC, APEDA quality programs are not bureaucratic exercises — they are the gatekeepers to billions of dollars of export revenue.
Modules 7–8 — Institutions and Procedures (Lectures 16–17):
APEDA, MPEDA, Commodity Boards, EIC, and the export documentation ecosystem are the operational machinery that converts India’s agricultural comparative advantage into actual export earnings.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
India’s Agricultural Export Vision 2030


The target: $100 billion in agricultural and food exports by 2030
The baseline: $43.7 billion (FY2024)
Required CAGR: ~12.5% per year over 6 years
Is it achievable? It is ambitious — but not impossible.
The strategic pillars for $100B:

Diversify beyond rice: Grow processed food, organic, millets, fruits, vegetables to reduce rice from 28% to below 20% of basket
New markets: Africa deepening, ASEAN penetration, India-UK FTA, India-EU FTA leverage
Quality investment: Cold chain (35M → 90M MT), testing labs, farmer GAP training — reduce post-harvest losses by 50%
Value addition: Processed, RTE, functional foods — double value per tonne exported
Leverage GI and branding: Premium products command 30–80% price premiums; scale GI exports
Reduce oil import dependence: Every 1M tonne of domestic oil palm production saves ~$700M in imports
Use FTAs strategically: CEPA, India-UK, India-EU — each 5–10% market access improvement matters



Scenario analysis:
Conservative (5% CAGR): $58B by FY2030 — achievable with current trajectory and no major policy changes.
Base case (8% CAGR): $69B by FY2030 — requires sustained cold chain investment, FTA benefits, and value addition push.
Ambitious (12% CAGR): $86B by FY2030 — requires all cylinders firing: processed food scale-up, millets breakthrough, organic exports boom, India-EU FTA in force.
$100B by FY2030: Feasible if India-EU FTA signed by 2026, processed food cluster investments bear fruit, and no major export restriction episodes damage trade credibility.

The honest assessment: $100B is a stretch target. $70B is realistic with right policies. Either way, the direction — toward higher value, more diversified, quality-compliant agricultural exports — is the correct one for India’s 600 million farming dependents.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
section: Final Thought: Agriculture and India’s Development
text:
Final Thought: Agriculture and India’s Development


India’s agricultural trade is not merely an economic topic. It is the story of 600 million lives.
Every percentage point improvement in agricultural export earnings — whether from a better price for Darjeeling tea, or a new market for Karnataka’s tur dal, or an organic certification that enables Madhya Pradesh soybean to command a premium in Hamburg — translates into:

Higher farm incomes for the cultivator who owns 1.08 hectares and whose children’s education depends on the kharif season’s outcome
More rural employment in post-harvest processing, cold chain logistics, packaging, and trade facilitation
Better food security through the forex earnings that fund India’s critical imports and stabilise the current account
National pride — in the Alphonso mango that Japanese consumers queue for, in the Coorg coffee that wins international awards, in the Indian ready-to-eat meal that a second-generation Indian in Leicester makes on a Tuesday night and calls it home

This is why international trade policy in agriculture matters.
And this is what ECO 2203 has been about.


A kilo of Kalaburagi tur dal travels from a Karnataka farmer to a diaspora kitchen in Leicester.
We began Lecture 16 with this image.
We can now trace every step:

The IEC and RCMC that licensed the export
The FOB contract and LC that financed it
The Phytosanitary Certificate and Shipping Bill that cleared it
The APEDA-linked FPO that aggregated the smallholders
The RoDTEP scrip that rewarded the exporter
The comparative advantage that made it competitive
The WTO rule that kept the market open

That journey is ECO 2203.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
section: Key Takeaways for the Examination and for Life
text:
Key Takeaways for the Examination and for Life

Five messages to carry beyond this classroom:

Comparative advantage, not absolute advantage, drives specialisation — India need not be the cheapest in everything; it only needs a relative cost advantage in what it exports. This is Ricardo’s enduring insight.
Free trade maximises efficiency, but food security requires nuanced policy — the tension between these goals is real, legitimate, and never fully resolved. The best economists acknowledge the trade-offs rather than pretending they do not exist.
WTO rules constrain AND protect India’s agricultural policy space — India must simultaneously defend its right to support farmers and work to ensure global rules are fair for developing country agriculture. Both are valid positions.
SPS compliance is the critical bottleneck for India’s agri export growth — not tariffs, not logistics, not finance. The moment India’s spice, seafood, or fresh produce consistently meets the safety standards of the EU, USA, and Japan, billions of dollars of additional export value become accessible.
Value addition, not raw commodity exports, is the path to $100 billion — India must move from exporting raw cashew to exporting cashew butter; from raw turmeric to curcumin capsules; from frozen shrimp to ready-to-cook seafood meals. That transition is the work of the next decade.

title: Lecture 18: India’s Agricultural Trade and Foreign Trade Policy
text:
Examination Information


Final Examination — ECO 2203:
Format:

Part A: 20 short-answer questions (2 marks each) — 40 marks
Part B: 4 essay questions from 6 choices (15 marks each) — 60 marks
Total: 100 marks; Duration: 3 hours

Emphasis areas (all lectures equally weighted):

Trade theory: comparative advantage, HO theorem, ToT
Tariffs, quotas, ERP calculations, optimal tariff
WTO Agreement on Agriculture: three pillars, amber/green/blue boxes
SPS Agreement: MRL, risk assessment, India’s challenges
Balance of Payments and forex: current account, J-curve
India’s trade policy: FTP 2023–28, RoDTEP, APEDA, MPEDA
Export procedures: IEC, Shipping Bill, LC, key documents

Numerical questions:
Be prepared to calculate: ERP, welfare effects of tariff (DWL), terms of trade, exchange rate pass-through.


Suggested revision strategy:
Week 1 before exam: - Re-read lecture slides for all 18 lectures - Focus on definitions and institutional names (APEDA, ICEGATE, RoDTEP, NRCP) - Practise the tariff welfare diagram from memory
Week 2 before exam: - Attempt 2015–2024 past paper questions - Practise numerical problems on ERP, ToT, DWL - Revise India-specific data: FY2024 export value, top products, WTO bound rates

A final word from your instructor:
You have worked hard across 18 weeks on a subject that matters — not just for your examination, but for understanding one of the most consequential policy domains in India’s development journey.
Thank you. Good luck. Go well.

title: Lecture 16: Export Procedures and Documentation
text:
Lecture 16
Export Procedures and Documentation
From Karnataka Farm to Leicester Kitchen






A kilo of Kalaburagi tur dal travels from a Karnataka farmer to a diaspora kitchen in Leicester, UK.
What stands between the farmer and the foreign consumer?

Registrations with four government agencies
Twelve documents crossing seven desks
Two financial instruments spanning two continents
One customs portal used by 1.4 million exporters

Today’s lecture answers: how does this actually work?


India’s agri export snapshot (FY2024):

Total agri exports: ₹3.61 lakh crore (~$43.7B)
Agricultural exporters registered: ~85,000
Ports handling agri cargo: 13 major + 200 minor
Average documents per shipment: 8–12

Every export begins with a registration and ends with a bank certificate.









Before any shipment, an agricultural exporter must hold:
1. Import Export Code (IEC) - Issued by DGFT (Director General of Foreign Trade) - 10-digit PAN-based code — mandatory for all exports - One-time registration; lifelong validity - Apply online at dgft.gov.in — fee ₹500
2. RCMC (Registration-cum-Membership Certificate) - Issued by the relevant commodity body: - APEDA — processed food, fresh produce, meat, dairy, cereals - MPEDA — marine products - Spices Board — all spices - Coffee/Tea/Rubber Board — respective commodities


3. GST Registration
GSTIN is mandatory for tax input credit on export supplies. Exports are zero-rated under GST — exporter can claim refund of input taxes.
4. AD Bank Account
Authorised Dealer (AD) Category-I bank account for receiving foreign exchange. FEMA requires all export proceeds to be realised through an AD bank within 9 months of shipment.




Sequence matters: IEC → GST → RCMC → AD Bank account. No RCMC without IEC. No export incentive claim without RCMC.







An export contract specifies: price, quantity, quality, delivery terms, payment mode, and dispute resolution.
Incoterms 2020 (ICC, Paris) — 11 standardised trade terms that define exactly where risk and cost transfer from seller to buyer.
Key terms for Indian agri exports:



Term
Risk Transfers At
Used For




EXW
Seller’s premises
Rare in agri


FCA
Named place to carrier
Air/rail shipments


FOB
On board vessel
Most Indian agri


CIF
Destination port
Buyer-preferred


DDP
Buyer’s premises
E-commerce niche





FOB vs CIF for India:
Under FOB, the Indian exporter’s responsibility ends when goods cross the ship’s rail. Freight and insurance arranged by the buyer.
Under CIF, the exporter arranges (and prices) freight and insurance to the destination port.
~65% of India’s agri exports are on FOB terms. CIF is preferred by Gulf and African buyers who have captive freight arrangements.
Negotiation tip: FOB is simpler for small exporters; CIF offers a higher invoice value and more price control.










Commercial Invoice — price, quantity, HSN code, IEC, buyer/seller details; basis for customs valuation
Packing List — box-by-box itemisation of weight, dimensions, marks
Bill of Lading (B/L) — title document issued by shipping line; original B/L needed to claim cargo
Airway Bill (AWB) — air freight equivalent of B/L; non-negotiable consignment note
Certificate of Origin (CoO) — issued by FIEO/chamber; determines FTA preferential tariff eligibility



The Bill of Lading is the only document that is a negotiable instrument — it can be endorsed and transferred like a cheque.




Detailed descriptions:

Commercial Invoice — price, quantity, HSN code, IEC, buyer/seller details; basis for customs valuation and bank payment verification under LC.
Packing List — box-by-box itemisation of weight, dimensions, marks and numbers; used by customs and buyer for checking receipt.
Bill of Lading (B/L) — shipping company’s receipt and title document; original needed to claim cargo at destination port; negotiable instrument that can be endorsed and transferred.
Airway Bill (AWB) — non-negotiable air freight equivalent of B/L; issued by airline; consignee named directly.
Certificate of Origin (CoO) — issued by FIEO, chamber of commerce, or APEDA; proves country of origin; required for preferential duty rate under FTAs.








Phytosanitary Certificate — issued by PPQS (Plant Protection, Quarantine & Storage); mandatory for all plant products
Health / Inspection Certificate — issued by EIC for meat, marine, organic, dairy exports
EDPMS declaration — RBI’s online system linking shipment to forex realization
Fumigation Certificate — mandatory for pulses, oilseeds; issued by certified fumigators
Shipping Bill — key customs clearance document filed on ICEGATE; must be approved before loading



Document flow for a typical agri export:

Export contract signed → Commercial Invoice prepared
LC opened by buyer’s bank
Shipment arranged → B/L issued
Phytosanitary + Fumigation certificates obtained
Shipping Bill filed on ICEGATE → LEO (Let Export Order) obtained
Documents presented to advising bank → payment
EDPMS updated → forex realization confirmed





Detailed descriptions:

Phytosanitary Certificate — issued by Directorate of Plant Protection, Quarantine & Storage (PPQS) under DAPFW; certifies products are pest-free; mandatory for all plant and plant-product exports.
Health / Inspection Certificate — issued by Export Inspection Council (EIC) for meat, marine, organic, and dairy exports; confirms product meets importing country food safety standards.
EDPMS declaration (Export Data Processing and Monitoring System) — RBI’s online system; all export shipments must be registered; links shipment data to forex realization within 9 months.
Fumigation Certificate — mandatory for pulses, oilseeds, and many cereals; issued by certified fumigators; many countries require methyl bromide or phosphine treatment; certificate must specify chemical, dosage, duration.
Shipping Bill — the key customs clearance document filed on ICEGATE; must be approved by Customs before loading; distinct types (Free Shipping Bill, Dutiable SB, SB under claim for Drawback) for different incentive claims.








Why LC? In international trade, buyer and seller do not know each other. The LC inserts two banks as guarantors.
Step-by-step LC mechanism:

Buyer and seller agree on price and LC terms
Buyer’s bank (Issuing Bank) issues LC in favour of exporter
Advising Bank (exporter’s bank in India) notifies exporter; may also confirm
Exporter ships goods; presents documents to advising bank
Documents checked against LC terms; bank pays exporter
Documents forwarded to issuing bank; buyer pays and gets documents
Buyer presents B/L to shipping line → collects cargo



Types of LC:

Sight LC: Exporter paid immediately on document presentation
Usance/DA LC: Payment deferred (30, 60, 90 days) — extends credit to buyer
Revolving LC: Renews automatically for repeat shipments
Back-to-Back LC: Small exporters use master LC as collateral to open LC for their suppliers

India’s EXIM Bank: Pre-shipment and post-shipment credit; refinances AD banks for agri exporters.
ECGC: Export Credit Guarantee Corporation — insures against buyer default and country risk. Premium: ~0.5–1% of invoice value.








library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

df_credit <- data.frame(
  Year = factor(
    c("FY2018","FY2019","FY2020","FY2021","FY2022","FY2023","FY2024"),
    levels = c("FY2018","FY2019","FY2020","FY2021","FY2022","FY2023","FY2024")),
  Pre_Shipment_B  = c(1.2, 1.3, 1.1, 0.9, 1.4, 1.7, 1.9),
  Post_Shipment_B = c(0.8, 0.9, 0.7, 0.6, 0.9, 1.1, 1.2)
)

df_long <- df_credit |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = c(Pre_Shipment_B, Post_Shipment_B),
                      names_to = "Type", values_to = "Value_B") |>
  dplyr::mutate(Type = ifelse(Type == "Pre_Shipment_B",
               "Pre-Shipment Credit", "Post-Shipment Credit"))

ggplot(df_long, aes(x = Year, y = Value_B, colour = Type, fill = Type, group = Type)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.15, position = "identity") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 3.5, xmax = 4.5, ymin = 0, ymax = 2.4, fill = "grey90", alpha = 0.4) +
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = 2.2, label = "COVID\ndip", size = 2.9, colour = "grey45", hjust = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Pre-Shipment Credit" = "#012169", "Post-Shipment Credit" = "#B9975B")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Pre-Shipment Credit" = "#012169", "Post-Shipment Credit" = "#B9975B")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 2.4), labels = function(x) paste0("$", x, "B")) +
  labs(title = "India's Export Credit: Pre vs Post Shipment (USD Billion)",
       subtitle = "Scheduled commercial bank credit outstanding for agricultural export finance",
       x = NULL, y = "USD Billion", colour = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))







Figure 1: Pre-shipment credit dipped during COVID (FY2021) and recovered alongside export growth; post-shipment credit tracks pre-shipment with a structural lag. Source: RBI, Handbook of Statistics on Indian Economy.











ICEGATE (Indian Customs EDI Gateway) — the single portal for all Indian customs transactions.
Shipping Bill types:



Type
When Used




Drawback SB
Claiming duty drawback on inputs


DEEC/Advance Auth SB
Against advance authorisation


EP Copy SB
Ordinary exports


Free Shipping Bill
Personal effects, gifts



Process flow: 1. Exporter files Shipping Bill online with all document details 2. Customs Risk Management System (RMS) assesses risk 3. Low-risk: Green Channel → LEO (Let Export Order) in 2–4 hours 4. Medium-risk: Yellow Channel → document examination 5. High-risk: Red Channel → physical examination of cargo


LEO — Let Export Order:
The LEO is the customs officer’s green light for loading. Cargo cannot board without LEO. Once LEO is given:

Exporter has 24 hours to load at port
Date of LEO = official date of export for FEMA and incentive purposes

AEO Status (Authorised Economic Operator):
Exporters with strong compliance history can apply for AEO status (Tier I, II, III). Benefits: - Fast-track customs clearance - Reduced physical examination - Priority in port queue - Deferred duty payment
In FY2024, ~12,000 entities hold AEO status in India.








library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

df_mode <- data.frame(
  Mode    = rep(c("Sea", "Air", "Land", "Other"), 2),
  Year    = rep(c("FY2022-23", "FY2023-24"), each = 4),
  Value_B = c(297, 67, 52, 21, 310, 71, 56, 25)
) |>
  dplyr::group_by(Year) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Share = round(Value_B / sum(Value_B) * 100, 1),
    Mode  = factor(Mode, levels = c("Other", "Land", "Air", "Sea"))
  ) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

ggplot(df_mode, aes(x = Share, y = Mode, fill = Year)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.72), width = 0.64) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(Share, "% ($", Value_B, "B)")),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.72), hjust = -0.07, size = 3.1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FY2022-23" = "#4a7fd4", "FY2023-24" = "#012169")) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 86), labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(title = "India's Merchandise Exports by Mode of Transport (FY2023)",
       subtitle = "Total exports FY2022-23 = USD 437 Billion  |  FY2023-24 = USD 462 Billion",
       x = "Share of Total Merchandise Exports (%)", y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40"))







Figure 2: Sea freight dominates at ~68% of India’s merchandise exports; air (~15%) and land (~12%) are secondary modes. Share composition is stable across both years. Source: DGCI&S / Ministry of Commerce and Industry, GoI.











Direct Export: - Exporter deals directly with foreign buyer - Higher margin, full control over branding/pricing - Higher risk (counterparty, forex, compliance) - Requires: IEC, RCMC, AD bank, full documentation - Suited to: large processors, established exporters
Examples: Kohinoor Foods (basmati direct to UAE retail), Venkateshwara Hatcheries (poultry to Middle East)

Indirect Export: - Sell to a Merchant Exporter (ME) who manages the export - No direct forex exposure; simpler for small farmer/processor - Lower price realisation (merchant takes margin) - ME provides GST exemption on purchase from manufacturer at 0.1% CGST+SGST
Export Management Company (EMC): manages exports on commission without taking title to goods.
FPO Route: ::: {.keybox} Farmer Producer Organisations (FPOs) aggregate smallholders (avg. 1.5 ha in Karnataka) to meet minimum export quantity. APEDA’s FPO Connect links 1,200+ FPOs to export markets. Tur dal FPOs in Gulbarga district now export directly to UK Indian grocery wholesalers.


:::






The Government of India recognises export performance tiers with Star Export House status, conferring priority clearances, bank credit, and EPCG benefits:



Status
Min. Export (3-yr avg)
Benefits




Export House
₹100 crore
Priority treatment


Trading House
₹500 crore
Self-certification of CoO


Star Trading House
₹2,000 crore
Fast-track customs, AEO


Premier Trading House
₹10,000 crore
All of above + policy voice





Major agri export houses (India):

ITC Agribusiness: wheat, spices, processed food; exports to 60+ countries; $1B+ annual turnover
Olam International: cashew, spices, coffee; integrated from farm to retail
Adani Agri Fresh: apple, potato, processed; cold chain integration
KRBL Ltd: world’s largest basmati rice brand (India Gate); exports to 90+ countries
Apex Frozen Foods: shrimp to USA/EU; vertically integrated aquaculture

These firms account for ~35% of India’s total agricultural export value.









1. RoDTEP (Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products) - Replaced MEIS in January 2021 (WTO-compliant) - Refunds embedded taxes not otherwise refunded: SGST, mandi tax, electricity duty, stamp duty - Agri rates: 0.5% to 4.3% of FOB value - Issued as transferable scrips on ICEGATE
2. Advance Authorisation (AA) - Duty-free import of inputs used in export production - Standard Input Output Norms (SION) define input ratios - Example: basmati rice exporter imports paddy from Nepal duty-free under AA
3. EPCG (Export Promotion Capital Goods) - Import capital goods (cold storage, processing equipment) at 0–3% duty - In return, commit to export 6× the duty saved over 6 years

4. Transport and Marketing Assistance (TMA) - Freight subsidy for agri exports to notified markets (Africa, EU, USA) - Cover: 50% of sea freight for small exporters; 25% for large - Products: fresh fruits/vegetables, processed food, organic, floriculture
5. MAI Scheme (Market Access Initiative) - APEDA funds participation in international trade fairs (Gulfood Dubai, SIAL Paris, Annapoorna Mumbai) - Buyer-seller meets, market studies, reverse buyer visits - Budget: ₹250 crore/year for all export promotion councils

WTO compliance note: RoDTEP is WTO-consistent (remission of domestic taxes, not export subsidy). AA and EPCG are permissible under WTO SCM Agreement as tax exemptions on inputs. TMA freight subsidy is under scrutiny — USA has raised concerns at WTO DSB.









FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)

Central licensing authority for all food businesses including exporters
An FSSAI Central Licence is mandatory for manufacturers/processors exporting food products
Annual turnover > ₹20 crore or multi-state operation → Central Licence

FSSAI’s role in exports:

Standards alignment: FSSAI standards increasingly aligned with Codex Alimentarius (facilitates market access)
Organic certification: FSSAI-accredited certification bodies issue Indian Organic Certificate (recognises NPOP)
Bilateral equivalence: FSSAI has equivalence arrangements with USDA NOP (organic) — US organic certifiers can certify Indian organic products



EIC accreditation for key markets:
The Export Inspection Council (EIC) issues health certificates that importing countries accept:



Market
Certificate
Requirement




European Union
EIC Health Cert.
Meat, fish, dairy


USA (FDA)
EIC + prior notice
Marine, processed


Japan
EIC + residue test
Marine, processed


Middle East
Halal + EIC
Meat, poultry



EIC operates 5 regional offices (Mumbai, Chennai, Kochi, Kolkata, Delhi) and 40 sub-offices. In FY2024, EIC certified ₹1.1 lakh crore worth of exports.

text:
Phytosanitary Certificate — issued by PPQS (Plant Protection, Quarantine & Storage); mandatory for all plant products
Health / Inspection Certificate — issued by EIC for meat, marine, organic, dairy exports
EDPMS declaration — RBI’s online system linking shipment to forex realization
